figures.caption: Table 1: SHD preprocessing and split used for all experiments.
Figure 1: Representative SHD test samples shown as raw 700-unit event rasters and the 140-by-70 pooled occupancy heatmaps used as model inputs.
Figure 2: On the left we show the held-out test-set class balance, and on the right we show the distribution of total occupancy per test sample.
Figure 3: Accuracy and loss curves for one fold of the cross-validation from both the membrane-readout and spike-readout training.

Table 3: Ten-fold precision sweep for the membrane-readout SNN. QAT is the fake-quantised PyTorch reference. HLS is hls4ml C simulation. Agreement is measured between HLS C simulation and the fake-quantised reference on the SHD test set.

Figure 4: Validation and test accuracy for the membrane-readout SNN. The FP32 PyTorch reference is shown together with the QAT reference and the generated hls4ml C-simulation result for each included fixed-point precision. Error bars and shaded regions show uncertainty calculated from 10-fold cross-validation.

Table 4: Hardware results for the membrane-readout SNN on xczu7ev-ffvc1156-2-e. Resource counts are from Vivado synthesis while clock, latency cycles, and initiation interval are from HLS synthesis. Full-window latency uses the requested target clock period.
Figure 5: Device utilisation after Vivado synthesis for the bit width sweep.

Table 5: NeuroBench workload metrics for the membrane-readout SNN on the SHD test set. Reported metrics are means and standard deviations over 10 folds. NeuroBench also reports accuracy metrics, but these matched the QAT results from the previous sections exactly.
